title: [2025] TRE 16
text:
IN THE JERSEY EMPLOYMENT AND
DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL 

 BETWEEN 

 ANDRA MARASTEANU 

 CLAIMANT 

 AND 

 LES AMIS LIMITED 

 RESPONDENT 

 INTERIM JUDGMENT 

 Reference: [2025]
TRE 16 

 Hearing
date: 23
July 2025 

 Before:
 Advocate
Dr Elena Moran, Chairman 

 For
the Claimant: In person 

 For the Respondent: Shaun Findlay, Managing
Director 

 DECISION 

 All
the claims are struck out for being out of time. 

 REASONS 

 1. 
 Andra Marasteanu was employed by Les Amis as a Support
Worker. Her employment started on 16 February 2022 and ended on 1 June 2025
after the current claim was submitted. The termination of her employment and
related matters are the subject of a separate claim. In this Claim Form she
alleges pregnancy and sex discrimination, unpaid wages and maternity rights. 

 2. 
 The purpose of the Interim Hearing is to determine if
any of the claims other than the unpaid wages claim were filed out of time. 

 Timeline 

 3. Ms Marasteanu
filed a witness statement as did her partner Keri Grey. Les Amis filed
statements from Shaun Findlay the Managing Director and Weronika Florek the
Head of HR. I was also provided with a bundle of documents that included
medical evidence and correspondence with JACS. 

 4. In determining
limitation in a strike out context the test is whether the Ms Marasteanu has
established a prima facie case. I must consider the case as it is pleaded in
the Claim Form and take it at its highest. I therefore assume that all the complaints
happened as described in the Claim Form. 

 5. 
 I find the timeline of key
events to be as follows: - 

 a. 
 One 19 July 2023 Ms
Marasteanu told her employer she was pregnant. 

 b. 
 Towards the end of October
2023 Ms Marasteanu considered making a complaint to her employer about all the
issues that had arisen in relation to her pregnancy to date. There was an
exchange of emails with the then Head of HR. On Monday 23 October 2023 the Head
of HR asked Ms Marasteanu to formalise her complaint.

 c. 
 On Friday 27 October 2023
Ms Marasteanu advised that she was in the process of collecting the
documentation and would have everything ready by the following week. 

 d. 
 On Monday 6 November 2023
Ms Marasteanu sent a further email to the Head of HR saying that she had had
some unexpected news during a scan and this together with other matters were
delaying her finalisation of the documents. She asked
for a further extension. The Head of HR asked her to submit the documentation
by 8 November 2023. Nothing was submitted. 

 e. 
 On 13 November 2023 Ms
Marasteanu was medically suspended from work on full pay. 

 f. 
 The baby was born on 20
January 2024. Ms Marasteanu received six weeks paid maternity leave. She
started unpaid maternity leave in early March 2024. 

 g. 
 In May 2024 Mr Gray was
signed off work with a knee injury. 

 h. 
 On 24 January 2025 Ms
Marasteanu approached JACS by email. 

 i. 
 On 28 January 2025 there
was a meeting with JACS. 

 j. 
 On 11 February 2025 Ms
Marasteanu had an email exchange with JACS. 

 k. 
 The Claim Form was
submitted on 14 February 2024. 

 JACS
advice 

 6. 
 Central to this case is the
advice on time limits that Ms Marasteanu was given by JACS. The initial contact
was by email on 24 January 2025. This resulted in a meeting with a JACS Senior
Adviser on 28 January 2025. The meeting was also attended by Mr Gray. The
Senior Adviser was asked to provide a witness statement for the hearing, but
she declined saying that JACS does not give witness statements for the Tribunal
process. She did provide a copy of an email exchange with Ms Marasteanu on 11
February 2025. 

 7. 
 Ms Marasteanu left the 28
January 2025 meeting with two pages of handwritten notes. I accept the evidence
of Ms Marasteanu that this is the handwriting of the Senior Advisor except for
three lines at the bottom of the second page which were written by Ms
Marasteanu. On the top right-hand corner of the first page the Adviser has
written her email address. Underneath appear the following words, “ 56 days
from 20 th Dec 14 February” . Underneath
are notes on how to fill in section 9 of the Claim Form and some suggested
wording. The second page is headed “ Disability Discrimination” and
contains some suggested wording for Mr Gray’s discrimination claim. All the
notes appear to relate to the reduction of a bonus that was paid on 20 December
2024. 

 8. 
 On 11 February 2025 Ms
Marasteanu sent an email to the Adviser attaching drafts of her claim and Mr
Gray’s claim. She says that they have decided to add multiple instances of
discrimination as they are connected and show the bigger picture. At the end of
the email she says “ I understand the tight timeframe left to file the
claims, last day being this coming Friday.” Friday would be 14 February
2025. The Adviser responded the same day. In her email she refers to the
addition of the other incidents and says, “ I appreciate as to why you have
provided the previous acts of discrimination however the law is very clear that
the claim needs to be lodged within 56 days of the act taking place.” T he
final sentence of the email reads, “Just be mindful, you are close to the 56
days for the claim to be lodged. Any further queries please do not hesitate to
contact me.” 

 9. 
 I accept the evidence of Ms
Marasteanu that at the meeting on 28 January 2025 she was advised in writing by
the JACS Senior Adviser that the limitation period for bringing a claim in
relation to the bonus paid on 20 December 2024 expired on 14 February 2025. This
was one day late as the claim should have been lodged by midnight on 13
February 2024. 

 Medical
evidence 

 10. 
 Ms Marasteanu says that
from the start of her employment at Les Amis in February 2022 she experienced
bullying which caused a decline in her mental health, panic attacks, time off
work due to work related stress and a prolonged period of counselling sessions.

 11. 
 In July 2023 she informed
Les Amis she was pregnant. Starting in September 2023 she had a series of fetal
scans the results of which were concerning. Her mental health continued to deteriorate,
she says, in part because of the way she was treated at work. In October 2023
she was referred to the Perinatal Mental Health Team due to the severity of her
condition which was exacerbated by the high-risk pregnancy and the stress at
work. 

 12. 
 Her pregnancy continued to
be difficult. There was an incident on 27 December 2023 when the baby’s
movements stopped. The birth in January 2024 was traumatic and she subsequently
experienced symptoms consistent with Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder. She was
placed under the case of a Mental Health Practitioner and started medication in
early May 2024. 

 13. 
 In May 2024 Mr Gray
underwent surgery and was signed off work until January 2025. She says that
this added stress as she became the sole caregiver for her child and her adult
partner who had serious mobility issues. In early May 2024 she began
experiencing pain related to stress that spread all over her body and she had
panic attacks for a period of several weeks. This means that she had to
postpone treatment for herself including therapy and pain medication. 

 14. 
 In June 2024 Ms Marasteanu
was referred to adult mental health services for suspected ADHS and Autism. 

 15. 
 A medical report dated 20
August 2024 records her historic difficulties and continuing pelvic pain and
mental health issues. 

 16. 
 A reference from the
Perinatal Mental Health Midwife dated 20 September 2024 records that Ms Marasteanu
is awaiting an assessment for ADHS and that her therapy has been postponed due
to having to care for her partner and child. 

 17. 
 A report from a Senior
Physiotherapist dated 4 October 2024 records Ms Marasteanu as presenting with
pelvic pain, birth trauma and multi-joint pain. 

 18. 
 A medical report dated 24
October 2024 records an improvement in mood but with residual anxiety and panic
attacks. Therapy sessions have started but have been postponed due to
difficulties with childcare. She is recorded as feeling burnt out and
everything being too much for her. 

 19. 
 An occupational health
report for Mr Gray dated 25 November 2024 says Mr Gray was at that time able to
walk without crutches. It records him as using an exercise bike and walking
when able. The report indicates that Mr Gray will most likely be able to return
to work on 12 January 2025. 

 20. 
 A letter from the Pain Service
dated 27 January 2025 records widespread body pain and continuing depression
and anxiety. A further letter from the Pain Service dated 9 April 2025 says
that Ms Marasteanu continues to have chronic widespread pain and pelvic pain. 

 Claims 

 21. The
Claim Form was received by the Tribunal on 14 February 2025. Ms Marasteanu alleges
pregnancy and sex discrimination, unpaid wages, maternity rights and
parental/adoption leave. She says on the Claim Form that the last act of
discrimination occurred on 20 December 2024. She has ticked no to the question
is the discrimination ongoing. 

 22. The
eight claims can be summarised as follows: - 

 A. The
reduction of a bonus paid on 20 December 2024 was sex discrimination and
unlawful deduction from wages. 

 B. A
poor performance review on 18 January 2024 was sex and pregnancy
discrimination. Ms Marasteanu only found out about the review in January 2025. 

 C. On
25 October 2023 Ms Marasteanu submitted a letter requesting work adjustments
which were not implemented. She was only suspended on health grounds on 13
November 2023. These actions were sex and pregnancy discrimination. 

 D. On
26 October 2023 Ms Marasteanu was put under pressure to take unpaid sick leave.
This was sex and pregnancy discrimination. 

 E. A
delay in undergoing a risk assessment until October 2023 was discriminatory on
the grounds of sex and pregnancy. 

 F. Between
July and November 2023 there was a delay in accommodating pregnancy related
needs which was discriminatory on the grounds of sex and pregnancy. 

 G. An
absence of suitable goals in her mid-year performance review in September 2023
was discriminatory on the grounds of sex and pregnancy. 

 H. The
Family Rights Policy only allowed 10 hours of paid time off for ante-natal
care. This was contrary to Article 55B of the Employment (Jersey) Law 2003 
and was discriminatory on the grounds of sex and pregnancy. 

 Limitation period 

 23. The limitation
period for discrimination claims is set out in Article 37 of the Discrimination
(Jersey) Law 2013 . 

 37 Making a complaint 

 (1) A person (the “complainant”) may present a
complaint to the Tribunal that another person (the “respondent”) has committed
an act, or is treated as having committed an act, which is prohibited by this
Law. 

 (2) The
Tribunal shall not consider a complaint – 

 ( a ) where the act complained of occurred
entirely before the coming into force either of this Law, or of Regulations
made under Article 5, amending this Law; 

 ( b ) unless it is presented to the Tribunal – 

 ( i ) before the end of the period of 8 weeks beginning with the date of the act, or the last act,
to which the complaint relates , or 

 ( ii ) within such further period as the
Tribunal considers reasonable in a case where it is satisfied that it was not
reasonably practicable for the complaint to be presented before the end of that
period of 8 weeks. 

 ( 3 ) For the purposes of this Article – 

 ( a ) any act prohibited by this Law which
extends over a period is to be treated as done at the end of the period; 

 ( b ) any
act which occurs before the coming into force of – 

 ( i ) this Law, or 

 ( ii ) Regulations made under Article 5, 

 and which continues
to occur on the date this Law or such Regulations come into force, is to be
treated as having occurred on the relevant coming into force date; 

 ( c ) where an act consists of a failure to do
something, that failure is to be treated as occurring when the person in
question decided on it. 

 24. 
 The limitation period for a
claim under Article 55B is set out in Article 55S of the Employment Law. 

 55S 
 Complaints to Tribunal for breach of requirement under Part 5A 

 (1) An
employee may present a complaint to the Tribunal that his or her employer has
contravened any requirement under Part 5A. 

 (2) No
complaint under this Article may be made in respect of a matter which has been
settled by agreement or withdrawn. 

 (3) The
Tribunal shall not consider a complaint under this Article unless it is
presented – 

 (a) before
the end of the period of 8 weeks beginning with the relevant date; or 

 (b) within
such further period as the Tribunal considers reasonable in a case where it is
satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the complaint to be
presented before the end of that period of 8 weeks. 

 (4) For the purposes
of paragraph (3), the “relevant date” mentioned in sub-paragraph (3)(a) is – 

 (a) in the
case of an alleged contravention of Article 55B(1), the date of the ante-natal
appointment referred to in either Article 55B(1A) or 55B(1B), or of the appointment
referred to in Article 55B(1C), whichever is the provision by virtue of which
Article 55B(1) applies in the particular case; 

 Are the claims in time? 

 25. 
 Article 37(2)(b)(i) of the Discrimination Law says
that a claim must be presented to the Tribunal “ before the end of the period of 8 weeks beginning with the date of the act, or the last act,
to which the complaint relates”. 

 26. 
 The Claim Form was presented to the Tribunal on 14
February 2025. For an act of discrimination that occurred on 20 February 2025 the
deadline for presenting the Claim Form to the Tribunal was midnight on 13
February 2025 (see Wharton v Barclays 1406-88/11 at paragraph 8). 

 27. 
 This means that complaint A relating to the payment of
the bonus on 20 December 2024 is one day out of time. Complaint B is over a
month out of time and complaints C to G are over a year out of time. Similarly,
the breach of Article 55B of the Employment Law is over a year out of time. In
other words, all the claims are out of time unless the Tribunal extends time
under Article 37(2)(b)(ii) of the Discrimination Law or Article 55S of the
Employment Law. 

 Reasonably practicable 

 28. To extend time the
Tribunal must be satisfied that it was not reasonably practicable for the
application to have been made before the end of the 8-week period. If it is
satisfied, the Tribunal must go on to consider whether the claim was issued
within such further period as the Tribunal considers reasonable. The burden is
on a claimant to satisfy the Tribunal that it was not reasonably practicable to
file within the eight-week period. 

 29. As to the test of
what is reasonably practicable the leading case, which has been consistently
applied by the Tribunal, is Skrzat v Jersey Telecom Group Limited 
1055-66/11. The claimant engaged a lawyer to advise him and submit his Claim
Form to the Tribunal. Due to a misconception on the part of the lawyer the
lawyer lodged the Claim Form one day late. The Tribunal struck out the Claim
Form. In paragraphs 11 and 12 the Tribunal said: - 

 11. The Tribunal
agrees with the approach of the Chairman in Jones v RBSI (above) that
the word “practicable” should be interpreted liberally, in favour of the
employee. However, in this case, the
reasons why it was apparently “not reasonably practicable” for the applicant to
file his JET1 in time – his limited understanding of English, his residence in
Poland – were in fact the very reasons why Mr Skrzat instructed Viberts to act
on his behalf in this matter. The
responsibility to file the form JET1 in time under the Law had passed to Viberts,
and would only have reverted back to Mr Skrzat if he had missed an unequivocal
deadline set (before the time limit under the Law) by Viberts, and this did not
occur in this case. 

 12. Article 76(2)(b)
allows the time limit set by Article 76(2)(a) to be extended by a reasonable
period if the Tribunal is satisfied that it was not “reasonably practicable”
for the form JET1 to be filed in the prescribed period of 8 weeks. This time limit of 8 weeks is stated in the
Law to provide a framework and certainty to all users of the Law. It is the Tribunal’s intention to construe
Article 76(2)(a) strictly and whilst the Tribunal is given a discretion to extend
that time limit (by Article 76(2)(b)) it will only do so in the most
exceptional circumstances for example where some highly unusual and unforeseen
event intervenes in the time between the EDT and filing the form JET1 at the
Tribunal office, or where an applicant genuinely did not know of the time limit
or his rights. Although this latter
example would be difficult to establish now that the Law is bedded down in
Jersey life and free, accessible advice is available from JACS and the CAB on
employment law issues. In this case, Mr
Skrzat instructed a law firm to act on his behalf. Mr Skrzat signed his form a full 10 working
days before the expiry of the time limit prescribed by Article 76(2)(a) of the
Law. It is a reasonable expectation that
a law firm will operate a diary system identifying all key time limits and
dates for filing papers in its clients’ affairs. In this case, an unfortunate error has been
made by Mr Skrzat’s lawyers in identifying a key date but that is not a
sufficient reason for the Tribunal to exercise the discretion given to it by
Article 76(2)(b). Quite simply, Mr
Skrzat’s form JET was filed out of time. 
 Mr Skrzat may have a claim against his lawyers, but his claim against
his former employers cannot proceed and it is HEREBY ORDERED that
Mr Skrzat’s complaint is STRUCK OUT of the Register of Complaints maintained by
this Tribunal. 

 30. In the UK the
courts have held that if a claimant misses a time limit because of negligent
advice by a lawyer the claimant cannot argue that it was not reasonably
practicable to file on time. A claimant wrongly advised by other types of
advisers may be able to obtain an extension. This line of authorities was
considered by the Tribunal in Ferreira v Waitrose (Jersey) Limited 
94/2013. In that case the claimant was four days late in submitting his claim
for unfair dismissal. He was correctly advised by JACS of the time limit but
wrongly advised by CAB that the employer would not take issue with the delay
because the employer had been late in providing documents. Despite the
erroneous advice of CAB, the Tribunal found that it had been reasonably
practicable for the claimant to file in time and the case was dismissed. 

 31. In relation to
medical matters the Tribunal has held that time only stops running against a
claimant if his or her medical conditions debilitate the claimant “ to a
point at which he was incapable of completing a form to stake his claims” ( Derouet
v Quantum Building Supplies Limited [2024 TRE 22). 

 32. I will consider
whether it was reasonably practicable for Ms Marasteanu to submit her claims
within the eight-week period starting with the five complaints that arose
between July and November 2023. These include C (failure to make reasonable adjustments
from 25 October 2023), D (pressure to take unpaid sick leave on 26 October
2023), E (delayed risk assessment until October 2023), F (delay in accommodating
pregnancy related needs between July and November 2023), G (absence of goals in
September 2023 mid-year review) and H (failure to allow time off for ante-natal
care). In each case the latest date that time would start to run would be 13
November 2023, i.e. the date she was suspended from work on health grounds. Taking
that date the deadline for submitting the claims would be midnight on Sunday 7January
2024. 

 33. 
 The test I must apply is whether her illnesses made
her incapable of completing a Claim Form. A Claim Form only requires basic
information; boxes to be ticked identifying the nature of the claims being made
and a very brief overview of the claims. There is no need to submit documents.
Ms Marasteanu was on the point of submitting a complaint to her employer in
November 2023. She says in her email dated 6 November 2023 that she is in the
process of finalising her complaints. She refers in her email to the unexpected
news from the scan and asks for additional time. I accept that this was a very
difficult time for Ms Marasteanu. She was suffering from depression and
anxiety, pelvic pain and going through a difficult pregnancy. I am not
satisfied that despite all these problems she was not capable to completing a
Claim Form. In her witness statement she says that after her suspension in
November 2023 she decided to postpone raising formal concerns indefinitely
until she considered that her and her baby were safe to do so. However, this
does not satisfy the test of being incapable of completing the Claim Form. I
therefore find claims C to H to be time barred. 

 34. In case I am wrong
on this I have also considered whether the submission on 14 February 2025 was
within a reasonable period. If Ms Marasteanu was incapable by reason of
ill-health from submitting her Claim Form before 7 January 2024, she arguably would
have remained incapable until at least the end of her paid maternity leave in early
March 2024. However, there came a point after that when her health problems and
the difficulties associated with looking after Mr Gray decreased. Mr Gray was
walking unaided by 25 November 2024. If I take that as the earliest point at
which she became capable of putting in a Claim Form she should have acted
quickly after that. However, she did not take any steps to progress matters. Her
first meeting with JACS was on 28 January 2025 more than two months later. By
this time the claims were more than a year old. If she wanted to pursue these
claims she should have taken urgent action after 25 November 2024 and submitted
the claims shortly afterwards. She did not do so and therefore the claims were
not submitted within a reasonable period as required by Article 37(2)(b)(ii). 

 35. Claim B is the
poor performance review which was conducted on 18 January 2024 without her
input or knowledge. It is not reasonably practicable for a claimant to submit a
claim about something they have no factual knowledge of and could not
reasonably have found out about. 

 36. Les Amis said at
the Interim Hearing that the HR system which includes the appraisal system
generates an automatic email every time an appraisal is updated. Les Amis were
unable to produce a copy of that email and Ms Marasteanu said that she never
received an email. I accept her evidence and find that she was unaware of the
performance review when it was done in January 2024. I therefore find that it
was not reasonably practicable for her to file her claim within the eight-week
time limit. 

 37. 
 Ms Marasteanu said at the Interim Hearing that she
first discovered the performance review at the end of January 2025 when she
logged onto the HR system to look for evidence for her claims. A print-out from
the Les Amis HR system shows that Ms Marasteanu logged onto the system on 3,
13, 23 and 31 January 2025. In an email sent to the Tribunal after the hearing
Ms Marasteanu confirmed that she
accessed the end of year review on 13 January 2025. 

 38. 
 The claim was filed on 14
February 2025 just over a month after she discovered the report. I do not
consider a delay of a month to be a reasonable period. Upon finding the report
she should have filed the claim within days not waited another month. As the
claim was not presented within a further period that I consider reasonable the
claim is time barred and is struck out. 

 39. Claim A relates to
the payment of a bonus on 20 December 2024. This claim was filed one day late.
I find that it was reasonably practical for Ms Marasteanu to file the claim in
time. I do so for two reasons. First, the fact that accrual of the bonus was
frozen during any period of unpaid leave was communicated to Ms Marasteanu via
email on 6 December 2024. The bonus itself was paid on 20 December 2024. She
had ample time from then to file the Claim Form by midnight on 13 February
2025. She had a meeting with JACS on 28 January 2025 and email communication on
11 February 2025. There was nothing preventing her from filing the Claim Form
on 12 or 13 February 2025. 

 40. Second, she was
wrongly advised by JACS that the last day for filing the claim was 14 February
2025. In Skrzat the Tribunal held that negligent action by a lawyer did
not make it impractical for the claimant to file on time. The service that was
provided by JACS was not dissimilar to the advice that might be provided by a
lawyer. Ms Marasteanu was given wording to put on the Claim Form and the
Adviser checked the draft Form for her. Her position was not materially
different from that of a claimant wrongly advised of the limitation period by a
lawyer. The one difference is that wrong advice from a retained lawyer might
give rise to a negligence claim against the lawyer. Advice provided by JACS has
partial immunity under Article 15 of Schedule 1 of the Jersey Advisory and Conciliation
(Jersey) Law 2003 . However, the lack of any effective redress against an
adviser will be a common situation in Jersey where many advisers do not carry
insurance. I do not consider that the lack of redress against JACS means that a
JACS adviser should be treated differently from a lawyer. 

 41. As it was
reasonably practical to file Claim A on time Ms Marasteanu is not entitled to
an extension. The claim is out of time and is struck out. 

 42. Had I found that
one or more of the claims was in time I would then have had to consider whether
any of the earlier discrimination claims were part of a continuing act under
Article 37(3)(a) of the Discrimination Law. As I have found that none of the claims were
submitted in time, I do not need to do this exercise. 

 Conclusion 

 43. All Ms
Marasteanu’s claims under the Discrimination Law and her single claim under the
Employment Law relating to ante-natal appointments were filed out of time and
are dismissed. 

 Advocate Dr Elena Moran, Chairman 
 Date: 31 July 2025 

 Page Last Updated: 07 Aug 2025
tribunal_members: Advocate Dr Elena Moran, Chairman For the Claimant: In person For the Respondent: Shaun Findlay, Managing Director DECISION All the claims are struck out for being out of time. REASONS 1. Andra Marasteanu was employed by Les Amis as a Support Worker. Her employment started on 16 February 2022 and ended on 1 June 2025 after the current claim was submitted. The termination of her employment and related matters are the subject of a separate claim. In this Claim Form she alleges pregnancy and sex discrimination, unpaid wages and maternity rights. 2. The purpose of the Interim
